ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tea food product class; no specific Earl Grey subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FHR covers tea infusions broadly; no Earl Grey-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance; no Earl Grey-specific concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot Earl Grey tea, approximately 240 ml, brewed without milk or sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, hot, prepared from leaf, without milk — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 14355)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for brewed black tea (plain, no milk, no sugar) used as the nutrient basis for Earl Grey tea. Earl Grey is black tea flavoured with bergamot oil; the bergamot flavouring contributes negligible macronutrients. Values represent plain brewed black tea per 100 g (approximately 100 ml). Bergamot oil is present in trace quantities and does not materially alter the macronutrient profile.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Plain brewed tea (black tea, no sugar): GI effectively zero; assigned GI=1 by convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates and has a GI effectively of zero. The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a numeric GI to zero-carbohydrate beverages. Per Project Veritas v2 convention for zero-calorie beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category is set to 'low'. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain hot Earl Grey tea is suitable for individuals with hypertension as it contains negligible sodium (approximately 7 mg per 240 ml cup) and no added sugar; however, patients should avoid adding high-sodium milk alternatives or excessive sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no impact on postprandial blood glucose when consumed without sugar or sweetened milk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot Earl Grey tea brewed without sugar or milk has zero carbohydrates and does not raise blood glucose, making it a suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should avoid adding sugar or high-carbohydrate sweeteners.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat and cholesterol; black tea polyphenols (flavonoids) may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot Earl Grey tea is free of saturated fat and cholesterol and may provide modest cardiovascular benefit through black tea flavonoids; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume it without full-fat milk or sugar to avoid unnecessary caloric and lipid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Earl Grey Tea (Hot) is a classic hot brewed tea made from black tea leaves flavoured with bergamot oil, the aromatic oil extracted from the rind of the bergamot orange (Citrus bergamia). It is one of the most widely recognised flavoured teas in the world, originating from British tea culture and named after Charles Grey, the 2nd Earl Grey and British Prime Minister in the 1830s. The tea is prepared by steeping Earl Grey tea leaves or a tea bag in freshly boiled water for three to five minutes, producing a fragrant amber-coloured infusion with a distinctive floral-citrus aroma and a slightly astringent, mildly bitter flavour profile. In Singapore, Earl Grey tea is served in cafes, Chinese restaurants, and hot pot establishments such as Hai Kah Lang, typically as a plain hot beverage without milk or sugar unless requested. Nutritionally, plain hot Earl Grey tea is essentially calorie-free, containing approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, with zero protein, zero fat, and zero carbohydrates. It contains trace amounts of potassium (37 mg per 100 g), magnesium (3 mg per 100 g), and sodium (3 mg per 100 g). Black tea contains polyphenolic antioxidants including theaflavins and thearubigins, which have been associated with modest cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits in observational studies. Earl Grey tea also contains caffeine (approximately 40–70 mg per 240 ml cup depending on steeping time and brand), which may be relevant for individuals sensitive to stimulants. The glycemic index is effectively zero as the beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates, making it entirely suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or high-fat milk. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher.
common_user_queries: Is Earl Grey tea good for diabetics?
How many calories in Earl Grey tea?
Is Earl Grey tea suitable for hypertension?
Does Earl Grey tea have carbohydrates?
What is Earl Grey tea made of?
Does Earl Grey tea contain caffeine?
Can I drink Earl Grey tea if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy brewed black tea (FDC ID 14355), which is the closest approved source. Earl Grey is black tea with bergamot flavouring; the bergamot oil contributes negligible macronutrients, making the adaptation highly defensible. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for Earl Grey tea was identified. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source is adapted rather than an exact match for Earl Grey specifically.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g (not zero) per USDA SR Legacy for brewed black tea; this reflects the trace energy from tea solids. 2. protein_g is naturally zero for plain brewed black tea with no milk added; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 14355. 3. fat_g is naturally zero for plain brewed black tea; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 14355. 4. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero for plain brewed black tea (no sugar added); confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 14355. 5. sugar_g is naturally zero for unsweetened plain brewed black tea. 6. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as tea is a liquid infusion with no fibre passing into the beverage. 7. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is low per Project Veritas v2 convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate_g is 0. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA source for plain brewed tea). 9. potassium_mg (37.0), sodium_mg (3.0), magnesium_mg (3.0), phosphorus_mg (1.0), iron_mg (0.02), zinc_mg (0.02), and riboflavin_mg (0.01) are reported from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 14355. 10. All remaining micronutrients set to 0.0 are confirmed negligible or zero per USDA SR Legacy for brewed black tea. 11. The serving size of 240 g (approximately 240 ml, one standard cup) is used for glycemic load calculation. 12. Bergamot oil flavouring does not materially alter the macronutrient profile of the tea. 13. This record is served at Hai Kah Lang - Funan Mall on GrabFood Singapore at SGD 4.96. 14. Cuisine is classified as British/International as Earl Grey tea is of British origin, though served in a Chinese hot pot restaurant context in Singapore.

improvement_actions_taken: Set glycemic_index to 1 (not 0 or null) per zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule.
Added zero-macro justification phrases in notes for protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g, sugar_g, and dietary_fibre_g.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'closest_parent' for all three ontology anchors.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Applied Singaporean multilingual naming rules (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given food is served in Singapore.
Documented all sentinel value usage; no -1 values used as all micronutrients were reportable from USDA source.
Set energy_kcal to 1.0 (not 0) to reflect actual USDA-reported trace energy in brewed black tea.
